system_prompt:
You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — single-note-soloing-vol-1

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

This book teaches jazz single-note soloing on guitar through a systematic chord-scale framework built from the ground up. Chapter 1 establishes the entire conceptual architecture: the major scale as the universal reference system, numeric scale-degree notation for describing both scales and chords, the chord formula system (expressing any chord as a pattern derived from the major scale, e.g., major 7th = 1, 3, 5, 7; minor 7th = 1, b3, 5, b7), and the governing operational concept that a chord "takes" a scale when that scale sounds good over it. The book's three organizing questions — what notes sound good over a chord progression, where those notes are on the instrument, and how to play them interestingly — structure the entire pedagogical arc from Chapter 1 through the final chapter.

The method is organized around named chord groups and their paired scales, introduced in a deliberate sequence. Chapter 1 establishes the first chord-scale rule: major Group 1 chords take either the major scale or the Lydian scale (the major scale with a raised 4th). Chapter 3 introduces the Dominant 7th scale (a major scale with a lowered 7th) and Chapter 4 extends this into Group-1 and Group-2 dominant families, adding the synchronization rule that runs containing the 3rd suit Group-1 dominants while runs containing the 11th suit Group-2 dominants. Chapter 4 also introduces the Overtone Dominant scale (Dominant 7th with a #4) and the Minor 7th scale (Major scale with lowered 3rd and 7th). Chapter 5 presents the Lydian Dominant / Overtone scale and the full taxonomy of Altered Dominant scales (Types 1, 2, and 3), defining altered dominants as dominant 7th scales containing one or more of b9, #9, b5, or #5/b13, and grouping any dominant chord built from those tones as GROUP 4 DOMINANT CHORDS. Chapters 6 and 7 realize these frameworks through fingerboard areas and extended register work.

A critical categorical boundary runs throughout the book: the Overtone Dominant, despite its #11, is explicitly NOT an altered dominant — the raised fourth reflects the overtone series rather than chromatic alteration. Similarly, a standing rule prohibits the relative-major shortcut for both Dominant 7th and Minor 7th scales, anchoring all thinking to root-based reasoning rather than positional transposition. These are not incidental warnings but structural rules that shape how the student internalizes scales on the instrument.

Greene's characteristic pedagogical moves recur across chapters. The chord formula system abstracts all chord and scale relationships into numeric patterns derivable from the major scale, giving students a single reference system rather than memorizing each chord type independently. The "combining scales in one position" technique — explicitly named in Chapters 4 and 5 — trains the player to move through multiple chord-scale relationships within a 5-6 fret fingerboard area rather than shifting position, with fingerboard visualization called out as the most important internalization practice. Register is treated as a primary compositional variable: Chapter 4 identifies whether extensions like b7 and 9 function as weighty bass tones or "spicy lower mid-range notes" as a function of voicing context, not just note choice.

Chapter 3 introduces the technical and rhythmic layer of the method. The eighth note is established as jazz's primary rhythmic unit; swing feel is defined as straight 8ths interpreted as triplet subdivisions with the middle note tied or missing; double-timing means running 16th notes at medium to fast tempos. A standing rule requires that all 8th-note patterns be practiced both swing and straight throughout the book. Slurring techniques — hammer-ons (constrained to scale tones at this stage), pull-offs, and trills — are introduced with a characteristic pedagogical reframe: the trill is decoration of a held note, not substitution of one note for another. The rule "clean playing before tempo increases" is stated explicitly.

Chapter 5 introduces polytonality as a named device: the upper-structure triad formed by the 9, #11, and 13 of the Lydian Dominant scale, rooted a whole step above the chord root, with a forward reference to a full chapter on the subject in Volume II. Greene's "package deal" approach — presenting all three altered dominant types side by side in the same fingerboard position so their color differences can be absorbed comparatively — is the method's answer to the problem of closely related scale types that would blur into one another if taught in isolation. A remedial rule accompanies this: students who cannot visualize the runs should return to the chord forms first, establishing chord form visualization as the prerequisite to scalar fluency. Chapters 6 and 7 are predominantly notated exercises, with load-bearing prose limited to structural scope rules ("There won't be any Type 3 - 4A... it's just the way the instrument works out"), substitution allowances (A7+ for A7#9+, D49 for D6/9), and standing practice rules such as trying every pattern an octave higher in the same position.

# Chapter 1 — FUNDAMENTALS: The Major Scale; Intervals; Chords; and Chord Tones

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

Chapter 1 establishes the foundational vocabulary and conceptual framework for jazz soloing, defining single-note soloing as playing successions of single notes that form melodies, and organizing instruction around three core questions: what notes sound good over chord progressions, where those notes are on the instrument, and how to play them interestingly. The chapter introduces the major scale as the reference system from which all other scales derive, and teaches the atomic building blocks—half-step and whole-step intervals—along with a numeric notation system (scale degrees 1-7) for describing both scales and chords. The master's characteristic move is the chord formula system, which expresses any chord as a numeric pattern derived from the major scale (e.g., major 7th = 1, 3, 5, 7; minor 7th = 1, b3, 5, b7), extended to upper partials (9ths, 11ths, 13ths), and paired with appropriate scales via the operational concept that a chord "takes" a scale when that scale sounds good over it. The chapter concludes with the first concrete chord-scale rule: major Group 1 chords typically take either the major or Lydian scale (the major scale with a raised 4th), establishing a pattern the method will build upon throughout.

# Chapter 2 — SOLOING OVER MAJOR TYPE CHORDS: The Major Scale; The Lydian Scale; How to Memorize Runs; Arpeggios; Position Playing and Left-Hand Fingering; Major Chord Forms; Major Chord Runs; Melody Notes; Fear Vs. Ear; Group-2 Major Chords and Soloing; Group-3 Major Chords and Soloing

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 is composed entirely of notated exercises with no extractable prose, covering major scale fingerings and foundational soloing techniques over major chord types (Major and Lydian scales, arpeggios, position playing and left-hand fingering, major chord forms and runs, melody notes, Fear vs. Ear, and Group-2/Group-3 major chord soloing) without presenting load-bearing conceptual passages.

# Chapter 3 — SLURRING and DECORATION - Part 1: Half-step Slides; Scale-Tone Slides; Pull-offs; Hammer-ons; Trills / TEMPO: 8th Notes, Triplets, 16th Notes and Double-timing

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 teaches slurring and decoration techniques—hammer-ons (constrained to scale tones in this stage of study), pull-offs, trills (reframed as ornamentation of a held note rather than substitution of one note for another), and their rhythmic contexts—alongside foundational tempo concepts including the eighth note as jazz's primary rhythmic unit, the swing feel (straight 8ths played as triplet subdivisions with the middle note tied or missing), and double-timing (running 16th notes at medium to fast tempos). Greene's characteristic moves visible here are the pedagogical reframe of the trill as decoration rather than substitution and the insistence on clean playing before tempo increases. Key terminology introduced: the Dominant 7th scale (a major scale with a lowered 7th tone), the scale-to-chord compatibility rule (any chord built from a scale's chord tones can take that scale), and a standing rule that all 8ths in the book be practiced both swing and straight. The chapter explicitly warns against the relative-major shortcut for dominant 7th scales, anchoring future learning to root-based thinking.

# Chapter 4 — THE DOMINANT 7th SCALE: Group-1 Dominants; Group-2 Dominants; Chord Forms; Dominant 7th Type Arpeggios and Runs; Melody Notes; Combining Scales in One Position

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 teaches the Dominant 7th scale and its application through Group-1 and Group-2 dominant chord families, establishing the synchronization rule that runs containing the 3rd suit Group-1 dominants while runs containing the 11th suit Group-2 dominants, with both/neither working over either group. Register is identified as a primary variable governing whether extensions like b7 and 9 sound like weighty bass tones or "spicy lower mid-range notes," a characteristic Greene move that subordinates note choice to voicing context. The chapter introduces the Minor 7th scale (Major scale with lowered 3rd and 7th) and warns again against thinking of it as a relative major a whole step lower, situating m7 practice within the ii-V-I progression. The Overtone Dominant scale is introduced concisely as a Dominant 7th with a #4. The chapter's organizing technique is combining Major and Dominant 7th scales within one position so the player can stay in a 5-6 fret area across multiple chords, with fingerboard visualization called out as the most important internalization practice.

# Chapter 5 — THE MINOR 7th SCALE: Minor 7th Chord Types; Chord Forms; Minor 7th Type Arpeggios and Runs; Melody Notes; Combining Scales in One Position

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Chapter 5 teaches the Overtone/Lydian Dominant scale and the three types of Altered Dominant scales as a unified package, defining an altered dominant as any dominant 7th scale containing one or more of the tones b9, #9, b5, or #5(b13), and establishing the taxonomy of GROUP 4 DOMINANT CHORDS as any dominant chord built from those altered tones. The chapter introduces POLYTONALITY as a named device — the upper-structure triad formed by the 9, #11, and 13 of the Lydian Dominant scale, rooted a whole step above the chord — flagging a whole later chapter on the subject in Vol. II. Greene's characteristic pedagogical move here is the "package deal": presenting all three altered types juxtaposed in the same position so the brain can absorb their subtle color differences side by side rather than in isolation, with an explicit remedial rule that students who struggle to visualize the runs should return to the chord forms first. A critical categorical boundary is drawn: the Overtone Dominant, despite its #11, is NOT an altered dominant — the raised fourth reflects the overtone series rather than a chromatic alteration.

# Chapter 6 — THE OVERTONE DOMINANT SCALE: Nature's Overtone Series; Group-3 Dominants; Chord Forms; Overtone Dominant Arpeggios and Runs; Combining Scales in One Position

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

Chapter 6 is dominated by notated exercises (arpeggios, scalar runs, and chord forms across the altered dominant Types 1, 2, and 3 in fingerboard Areas 2-4) with brief marginal prose directives rather than expository passages. The load-bearing prose consists of standing practice rules: a substitution allowance (A7+ for A7#9+, D49 for D6/9) that applies across all subsequent Type 3 pages, instructions to finger Em9 runs in the 5th position as well, a note that arpeggio tones can move to the 5th and 4th strings, and a left-hand technique reminder against tip-of-finger placement. Greene's characteristic move visible in the chapter is the bracketed, hand-annotated style of pedagogy — terse rules attached to specific exercises rather than discursive explanation — which assumes the framework established in earlier chapters and trusts the student to apply it. No new theoretical terminology is introduced; the chapter is the practical realization of the Overtone Dominant / Group-3 framework defined earlier.

# Chapter 7 — ALTERED DOMINANT SCALES (TYPE 1, 2, and 3): Altered Dominant Chord Types; Chord Forms; Altered Dominant Arpeggios and Runs

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

Chapter 7 continues the altered dominant program from Chapter 5 into successive fingerboard areas (Areas 4A, 5, 5A, and a higher-register supplement), dominated by notated exercises with brief marginal prose directives. The substantive prose establishes structural rules specific to the guitar — notably "There won't be any Type 3 - 4A.. it's just the way the instrument works out," a structural boundary preventing students from expecting nonexistent material — and the #9 deployment rule allowing the altered #9 to substitute for or sit alongside the natural 9. Greene's characteristic move here is to mark explicit scope boundaries (a "this type of thing will be discussed in Vol. II" forward-reference) and a standing practice rule that every pattern should be tried an octave higher in the same position. The chapter closes with EXTRA ARPEGGIOS FOR HIGHER REGISTER, extending the altered dominant patterns upward.

# Chapter 1 — Getting Started

_Pages 6-10._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Single-Note Soloing defined

> ‘Single-Note Soloing’ is another way of saying ’Single-Note Playing’,
> which means playing one note at a time or, more accurately, playing successions of single notes, which form
> melodies.

**Significance (p. 6).** Foundational definition of the method's central concept.

## Three core questions of soloing

> What notes can you play, especially when someone else is playing the chords behind you and you
> are doing the soloing, in other words, WHAT NOTES OR SCALES SOUND GOOD OVER ANY
> GIVEN CHORD PROGRESSIONS?
>
> 2 WHERE ARE THESE NOTES ON THE INSTRUMENT? and
>
> 3 HOW CAN YOU MAKE THESE NOTES SOUND INTERESTING? (This mainly involves being
> able to play these notes in many beautiful, flowing combinations.)

**Significance (p. 6).** Three-question framework structural spine of the method.

## Scale defined

> A scale is a fixed group of notes, usually built in an ascending direction from a fixed starting note.

**Significance (p. 7).** Book's working definition of 'scale.'

## Major scale as foundation

> In jazz, as in many types of music, the scale known as the MAJOR SCALE is used as a foundation
> from which we can build all other scales.

**Significance (p. 7).** Establishes major scale as reference system for all derivations.

## Half-step and whole-step intervals

> The most basic type of interval is known as the HALF-STEP. This refers to
> the distance between two adjacent notes... forinstance: A to Bb, Bd to B, B to C, C# to D, Eto F, and Gto Ad,
> are all examples of the half-step interval. The other basic type of interval is the WHOLE-STEP, which is
> simply a distance of two half-steps . . . some examples of whole-step intervals are: A to B, Bb to C, B to C#,
> D to E, E to F#, and Go to Ab.

**Significance (p. 8).** Atomic interval units from which all formulas derive.

## Scale degrees as numbers

> The different notes in a major scale are often referred to by number, for instance, if you see the statement
> "G is the 4th of D”, this means ’G is the 4th note in the D major scale.” This number business also applies
> to intervals.

**Significance (p. 8).** Establishes numeric notation system for chord formulas.

## Chord tones defined

> chord
> tones are simply the notes in a chord (the words "tone,” and “note” are often used interchangeably . . .
> also the word “degree”... Examples: "the tones in a chord,” “the notes in a chord,” and "the degrees of
> a chord” all mean the same thing).

**Significance (p. 9).** Defines chord tones and interchangeable terminology.

## Chord formula system

> All chords
> have a certain group of tones called a formula, that is derived from the major scale. For example, major
> 7th chords have the following formula: 1, 3, 5,7... this means that the chord tones of an A Major 7th
> chord would be the Ist, 3rd, Sth and 7th tones of the A Major scale, or: !A, 3C#, SE and 7G#. And the notes
> in an Eb Major 7th chord would be 'Eb, 3G, 5Bb and 7D (which are the Ist, 3rd, Sth and 7th tones of the
> Eb Major scale). Make sure you understand this before going any further.

**Significance (p. 9).** Introduces chord formula system — core analytical method of the book.

## Minor 7th chord formula

> Minor 7th chord has the following formula: 1, 63, 5, 67. (Note: the symbol ”b”
> placed before any tone in a formula means you Jower that tone by a half-step. The symbol "#” placed before
> any tone in a formula means you raise that tone by a half-step).
>
> So aC Minor 7th chord would contain the following tones: 1C,'3Es, 5G and *7Bé, which are the Ist,
> flatted 3rd, Sth and flatted 7th tones of the C Major scale.

**Significance (p. 9).** Worked example of formula system with altered tones.

## Triads — four types and formulas

> most basic kind of chord in music is called a triad; it is a chord that almost always has some type
> of 1, 3 and 5 in its formula. For instance, the Major triad’s formula is 1, 3, 5; the Minor triad’s is 1, 63, 5;
> the Diminished triad’s is 1, 63, 65; and the Augmented triad’s is 1, 3, #5.

**Significance (p. 9).** Catalogues all four basic triad types.

## Upper partials: 9th, 11th, 13th chords

> expanded to 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 and 11, you are talking about some type of eleventh
> chord. And when you expand it to 1, 3,5,7,9, 11 and 13, you are talking about some type of thirteenth chord.
>
> Notice that an I 1th is the same letter name as the 4th, and the 13th tone is the same letter name as the
> 6th. Once again the difference between the lower and upper partials is 7 (4 from 11 is 7, 6 from 13 is 7).

**Significance (p. 10).** Establishes upper-partial extension system.

## Chord takes a scale — terminology

> certain kind of chord “takes” a certain scale, what they mean is that that particular scale
> sounds good when played with that particular chord.)

**Significance (p. 10).** Defines the chord-scale pairing operational concept.

## Major Group 1 chords and scales

> group 1 major chords most commonly take one of two scales, either the MAJOR
> or the LYDIAN. As to which of the two to use, it is almost always a matter of personal preference . . . they
> both sound good in most cases

**Significance (p. 10).** First concrete chord-scale rule in the book.

## Lydian scale construction

> he Lydian’s, it is simply a Major scale
> with a #4th instead of the regular 4th. Example:
>
> The D Major scale has the following note: —————————>= !D ?E 3F# ‘G 5A 6B 7C# &(D)
> The D Lydian scale has the following notes: ——__—_>_ 'D 2E 3F# G# 5A 6B ICH 8(D)

**Significance (p. 10).** Defines Lydian via single difference from major scale.

# Chapter 3 — SLURRING and DECORATION - Part 1: Half-step Slides; Scale-Tone Slides; Pull-offs; Hammer-ons; Trills / TEMPO: 8th Notes, Triplets, 16th Notes and Double-timing

_Pages 41-60._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Hammer-on scope rule

> NOTE: This higher note, for now, will be
> found in the scale you are working with at
> that moment.

**Significance (p. 41).** Constrains hammer-on targets to scale tones at this stage of study.

## Hammer-ons in jazz context

> With the exception of a special useage known as the grace note, (to be covered in Volume II), hammer-ons,
> by themselves, have not been commonly used by most jazz guitarists. But they are commonly used as
> part of the device known as the frill, which will be discussed next.

**Significance (p. 41).** Positions hammer-ons as a building block for the trill rather than a standalone jazz device.

## Trill as decoration

> Actually, another way to look at this, and a way that I prefer, is that A is not really being
> replaced, but instead, is just being decorated, you know, like we're kind of playing around with the A note,
> but not getting rid of it, just enhancing it.

**Significance (p. 41).** Conceptual reframe of the trill as ornamentation rather than substitution, shaping student intent.

## Trill placement

> The trill is more commonly used on the strong part of the beat than the weak one, but experiment and see
> what you like.

**Significance (p. 41).** Stylistic rule for where in the beat the trill lands while leaving room for personal taste.

## 8th note as jazz unit

> At medium and fast tempos, which most of the runs in this book are intended for, the normal unit of
> rhythm in jazz is the 8th note. Great players often create a very fluid sound by running long streams of 8th
> notes together in measure after measure during one of their good solos.

**Significance (p. 39).** Establishes the eighth note as the fundamental rhythmic building block for jazz soloing.

## Swing 8ths definition

> In the swing feel, each group of two 8th notes is actually played as if it were a group of three triplets,
>
> with the middle one tied or missing. The ear then only hears two notes, which it interprets as kind of funny
> 8th notes.

**Significance (p. 39).** Core definition of swing feel: written straight 8ths transformed via triplet subdivision in performance.

## Practice both feels

> all the 8th notes in this book should be played as jazz 8ths and straight 8ths,
> because both feels are necessary to today’s music.

**Significance (p. 39).** Standing practice rule requiring every run be rehearsed in both swing and straight feels.

## Double-timing defined

> Players who possess great technique often love to break into 16th notes at medium (or even fast!)
> tempos; this is commonly referred to as double-timing.

**Significance (p. 40).** Names and defines double-timing as a specific advanced rhythmic technique.

## Speed comes from clean play

> The speed that is required here comes of course, as a result of constantly pushing yourself a little,
> gradually increasing the tempos on all the runs you are practicing; but also by making sure you are playing
> cleanly at any tempo before moving on to a faster one.

**Significance (p. 40).** Method's rule that cleanliness must precede tempo increases when developing technique.

## Dominant 7th scale defined

> The Dominant 7th scale is simply a Major scale
> with a lowered 7th tone (this tone will be symbolized by the following: 57).

**Significance (p. 41).** Foundational definition of the dominant 7th scale as a major scale with a lowered 7.

## b7 notation note

> Notice that the term ”57” means that the 7th tone is lowered one-half-step, NOT that the lowered 7th
> tone always ends up being a “flat” note.

**Significance (p. 41).** Prevents misreading of chord formula notation by clarifying what 'lowered' means in spelling.

## Scale-chord compatibility rule

> Any chord containing some combination of any of (but only) these chord tones, can take the Dominant
> 7th scale.

**Significance (p. 41).** Core scale-to-chord matching rule defining the dominant chord types compatible with the scale.

## Avoid relative-major shortcut

> Don’t fall for
> the “oh, dom. 7th scales, they're really just major scales up a 4th-trap” — it will only tend to make things
>
> harder later if you do.

**Significance (p. 43).** Pedagogical warning against the relative-major shortcut; dominant 7th must be internalized from its own root.

# Chapter 4 — THE DOMINANT 7th SCALE: Group-1 Dominants; Group-2 Dominants; Chord Forms; Dominant 7th Type Arpeggios and Runs; Melody Notes; Combining Scales in One Position

_Pages 61-85._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Run/chord group sync

> for maximum synchronization of runs and chords, you could generalize and say that if a run
> contains the 3rd, use it over any of the group-1 dominants, and if it contains the 11th, use it over any of the
> group-2 dominants. And if a run contains both (or neither) the 3rd and 11th, use it over any chord in
> either group of chords. But watch out for ending or lingering tones.

**Significance (p. 59).** Core rule for matching runs to dominant chord types via scale content.

## Register importance for b7 and 9

> If you said REGISTER, you were on the money. 67 and 9 really start to come alive in this “higher” register (it's still a fairly low
> register, but high enough so that these two tones don't sound like big, weighty bass tones, but rather, more like spicy lower mid-
> range notes).

**Significance (p. 58).** Teaches that register governs how chord extensions sound — foundational for voice leading.

## Combining scales in one position

> On the following pages you will see examples of runs that combine the Major and Dom. 7th scales, in
> one position at a time. This type of guitar playing is a little preview to what happens when you have to solo
> over a lot of chords in one song or piece. You will want to know separate scales for the different chords in
> the song, but if you know enough positions of each scale, you will be able to stay in one little 5 or 6 fret area
> and play through the whole song

**Significance (p. 60).** Defines the method's position-based approach to multi-chord soloing.

## Visualize fingerboard

> Remember to
> VISUALIZE the notes on the fingerboard as discussed earlier; at the risk of boring you, let me say it again:
> THIS IS VERY IMPORTANT! It is one of the best things you can do for yourself.

**Significance (p. 67).** Primary technique rule for internalizing scale positions — visualization over rote patterns.

## Minor 7th scale defined

> The Minor 7th scale is simply a Major Scale with
> LOWERED 3rd (63) and LOWERED 7th (67) tones.

**Significance (p. 66).** Core definition of the minor 7th scale.

## Warning: don't think m7 as relative major

> You will probably notice that each m7 scale has the same notes as a major scale whose root is a whole
> step lower. But, as cautioned before on dom. 7 scales, for your own good, don’t think of the m7 scale in
> this way now.

**Significance (p. 69).** Pedagogical rule against the relative-major reframe — keeps focus on root-based chord-tone thinking.

## ii-V-I context for m7 runs

> This particular series of sounds is one of the most common in jazz (it is known as “ii - V - I’ if
> that means anything to you now; if not that’s O.K.., it will be covered later in Volume II), and is a practical
> way, at this stage of the game, for you to become acquainted with m7 runs.

**Significance (p. 67).** Situates m7 exercises within the ii-V-I progression — the most structurally important sequence in jazz.

## Overtone Dominant scale defined

> The Overtone Dominant scale is simply a Dominant 7th scale with a #4th tone instead of the regular 4th.

**Significance (p. 82).** Concisely defines Overtone Dominant by its single distinguishing feature.

## Jazz Waltz feel

> 3/4 time can be very attractive in jazz, creating the feel known as "Jazz Waltz". You may find it highly stimulating for variety's sake, to make up some runs in 3/4 time, as above, or even just using the Major (or Lydian) scale, or just the Dom. 7 scale.

**Significance (p. 71).** Introduces Jazz Waltz as a creative-application rule for learned scales across meters.

# Chapter 5 — THE MINOR 7th SCALE: Minor 7th Chord Types; Chord Forms; Minor 7th Type Arpeggios and Runs; Melody Notes; Combining Scales in One Position

_Pages 86-105._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Lydian Dominant naming

> Because it contains the #4th (#11th), the Overtone Dominant scale is also called the LYDIAN
> Dominant scale.

**Significance (p. 83).** Defines the dual name of a key scale and the structural reason behind it.

## Upper-structure triad

> An interesting feature of this scale and also the Lydian scale
> is that the three “hippest” notes (namely, the 9, #11 and 13) form a major triad whose root is a whole step
> above the root of the scale.

**Significance (p. 83).** Teaches the upper-structure triad — foundational for polytonal color over Lydian Dominant.

## Polytonality defined

> This type of thinking and sound is commonly known as POLYTONALITY, on which there will be
> a whole chapter in Volume II.

**Significance (p. 83).** Names polytonality as a deliberate improvisational device.

## Altered dominant scale defined

> What is an altered dominant scale? Simply, any dominant 7th scale with one or
> more of the following tones: 59, #9, 65 or #5 (613).

**Significance (p. 94).** Definitive rule for what qualifies as an altered dominant scale — gateway to the section.

## Group 4 dominants

> Any Dom. 7th type chord that contains any one or more of the four altered tones (69, #9, 65 and
> #5 or 613) will be considered as being part of what we call the GROUP 4 DOMINANT CHORDS.

**Significance (p. 94).** Establishes taxonomy linking altered-tone chords to Group 4, connecting chord selection to scale choice.

## Package-deal learning rationale

> the three types of A.D. scales are all so
> similar that your brain, while noting the differences, can absorb them all in a kind of ‘package deal’, one
> position at a time. Also because they are all juxtaposed in the same position, the subtle differences betwen
> them will be more evident to you, thereby increasing your sensitivity to these slightly different colors, and
> helping you to learn the sounds faster.

**Significance (p. 96).** States the pedagogical method of presenting all three altered types in the same position simultaneously.

## Visualizing via chord forms

> If you have trouble visualizing any of these runs, then please, for your own good, go back and learn the chord forms (group 4) for this position. This should straighten it out.

**Significance (p. 99).** Remedial rule: when runs are hard to visualize, return to chord forms first.

## Overtone Dom not altered

> The Overtone Dominant scale is not considered to be an altered dominant scale — that #11 is not
> really a b5th, even though we may call it that — check the Overtone Series diagram again if you are in
> doubt

**Significance (p. 94).** Critical categorical boundary between Overtone Dominant and altered dominants.

# Chapter 6 — THE OVERTONE DOMINANT SCALE: Nature's Overtone Series; Group-3 Dominants; Chord Forms; Overtone Dominant Arpeggios and Runs; Combining Scales in One Position

_Pages 106-120._

If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Type 3 chord substitution rule

> As before, and on all Type 3 altered dominant pages coming up, you may wish to try A7+ for A7#9+
> and D49 for D6/9.

**Significance (p. 105).** Standing chord-substitution rule applied across all Type 3 altered dominant exercises.

## Practice in alternate position

> Try fingering all these Em9 runs in the Sth position too.

**Significance (p. 109).** Standing technique instruction to practice the same material in an alternate position, expanding positional fluency.

## Arpeggio string flexibility

> These notes may be played on other strings too (5th & 4th strings)

**Significance (p. 107).** Teaches string-choice flexibility when voicing arpeggio notes.

## Left-hand placement reminder

> REMINDER:
> not on tip of finger

**Significance (p. 111).** Left-hand technique reminder against tip-of-finger placement, correcting a common error.

## Type 3 area-4A absence

> There won't be any Type 3 - 4A.. it’s just the way the instrument works out.

**Significance (p. 118).** Structural rule defining the boundaries of the Type 3 system on the guitar.

## #9 substitution rule

> INSTEAD OF OR IN ADDITION TO THE #9

**Significance (p. 131).** Performance rule: the altered #9 can substitute for or supplement the natural 9.

## Vol II deferred

> discussed in Vol. II.

**Significance (p. 131).** Marks deliberate scope boundary — certain techniques deferred to a later volume.

## Practice octave higher

> Also try this run an octave higher in the same position.

**Significance (p. 120).** Pedagogical rule: every pattern should be practiced an octave higher in the same fingerboard position.

## Higher-register arpeggios

> EXTRA ARPEGGIOS FOR HIGHER REGISTER

**Significance (p. 133).** Supplementary section providing arpeggios extending altered dominant patterns into the upper register.
